title: From a signal to a covariance matrix

screens.prompt: Case update: instead of one number at a time, you now stack each sample of the recording with its neighbour into a 2D vector $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1)]^T$. If the underlying process has a slowly-varying envelope (like your recurring burst), are the two entries of $\mathbf{x}(n)$ independent of each other?
choices: Yes — x(n) and x(n-1) are separate samples, so they're independent
No — a slowly-varying signal makes neighbouring samples correlated
screens.reveal: They're correlated — that's exactly what "slowly varying" means: knowing $x(n-1)$ tells you something about $x(n)$. That correlation is captured by the **covariance matrix** of the vector $\mathbf{x}(n)$: $\Sigma = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)^T]$, a 2x2 symmetric matrix whose off-diagonal entry is exactly $R_x(1)$, the autocorrelation at lag 1 from an earlier phase of this case.

screens.prompt: For $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1)]^T$ from a zero-mean wide-sense stationary process, what are the entries of $\Sigma = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)^T]$?

choices.feedback: The diagonal entries are E[x(n)x(n)] and E[x(n-1)x(n-1)] — both lag-0 autocorrelations, R_x(0). The off-diagonal is the lag-1 cross term.

choices.text: All four entries equal the process variance
choices.feedback: Only the diagonal entries equal R_x(0) (the variance, for zero-mean); the off-diagonal entries carry the lag-1 correlation, which is generally different unless the process has no memory at lag 1.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — stacking any WSS process into a lag vector always produces a symmetric covariance matrix built entirely out of the autocorrelation function values you already know how to compute.
